title: Single-Cell Omics Tutorials
text:
What’s Covered
This tutorial series walks through complete single-cell omics analysis workflows, from raw data ingestion to publication-ready figures.

title: Single-Cell Omics Tutorials
text:
🧬 scRNA-seq
Using Seurat v5 as the primary framework.



Chapter
Topic




01
QC & Filtering


02
Normalization & HVG Selection


03
PCA · UMAP · tSNE


04
Graph-based Clustering


05
Cell Type Annotation


06
Differential Expression + ComplexHeatmap


07
Trajectory Analysis (Monocle3)


08
Batch Integration (Harmony)





🔓 scATAC-seq
Using ArchR for project management and peak analysis.



Chapter
Topic




01
QC & Arrow File Creation


02
LSI · UMAP


03
Peak Annotation & Gene Scores


04
TF Motif Analysis (chromVAR)







🗺️ Spatial Transcriptomics
Using Seurat with 10x Visium data.



Chapter
Topic




01
Loading & Spot-level QC


02
Spatially Variable Genes


03
Cell Type Deconvolution





🔗 Multi-omics & Genotyping
Integration strategies and donor demultiplexing.



Chapter
Topic




01
WNN Integration (RNA + ATAC)


02
Signac: Linked Peaks to Genes


03
Donor Demultiplexing

title: Single-Cell Omics Tutorials

Software Versions
Tutorials are developed and tested with the following versions:








Package
Version
Purpose




R
≥ 4.3.0
Base environment


Seurat
5.x
scRNA-seq, Spatial, WNN


ArchR
1.0.3
scATAC-seq


Signac
1.13.0
Chromatin accessibility


Monocle3
1.3.x

title: Single-Cell Omics Tutorials
text:
How to Use These Tutorials
Each chapter follows a consistent structure:

Overview — biological rationale and goals
Setup — required packages and data
Code walkthrough — annotated, reproducible R code
Parameter guidance — when and how to adjust defaults
Checkpoints — saveRDS() calls to preserve progress
Session info — exact package versions used







TipReproducibility



All tutorials use execute: freeze: auto in Quarto, meaning outputs are cached after the first render. Enable this by committing the _freeze/ directory to your repo.

title: Getting Started
text:
Prerequisites
These tutorials assume:

title: Getting Started
text:
R ≥ 4.3.0 installed (or access to an HPC with R modules)
Familiarity with R syntax and Bioconductor
Basic understanding of single-cell library preparation

title: Getting Started
text:
Environment Management with renv
We strongly recommend using renv to lock package versions per project, ensuring reproducibility across machines and time.
install.packages("renv")
renv::init()       # initialise in your project directory
renv::snapshot()   # lock current package versions to renv.lock
renv::restore()    # restore from renv.lock on another machine

title: Getting Started
text:
Install Core Packages

Seurat + Harmony + Monocle3
# Seurat v5
install.packages("Seurat")
install.packages("SeuratData")

# Harmony
install.packages("harmony")

# Monocle3 dependencies (Bioconductor)
if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE))
    install.packages("BiocManager")

BiocManager::install(c(
  "BiocGenerics", "DelayedArray", "DelayedMatrixStats",
  "limma", "lme4", "S4Vectors", "SingleCellExperiment",
  "SummarizedExperiment", "batchelor", "HDF5Array",
  "terra", "ggrastr"
))

devtools::install_github("cole-trapnell-lab/monocle3")


ArchR (scATAC-seq)
devtools::install_github(
  "GreenleafLab/ArchR",
  ref   = "master",
  repos = BiocManager::repositories()
)
ArchR::installExtraPackages()






Note



ArchR installation requires devtools. On HPC systems, you may need to load a git module before running devtools::install_github().




Signac (scATAC / Multi-omics)
BiocManager::install(c(
  "BSgenome.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg38",
  "EnsDb.Hsapiens.v86",
  "TFBSTools",
  "JASPAR2020"
))
install.packages("Signac")


Visualization
BiocManager::install("ComplexHeatmap")
install.packages(c(
  "ggplot2", "patchwork", "ggrepel",
  "viridis", "RColorBrewer", "scales",
  "cowplot", "ggsci"
))

title: Getting Started
text:
Running on HPC
If you are working on an HPC cluster (as most of these workflows require), load modules before starting R:
module load R/4.3.3
module load gcc/11.2.0    # required for some package compilation
module load hdf5           # required for ArchR / HDF5Array
For memory-intensive steps (e.g., ArchR Arrow file creation, Harmony integration), request adequate resources:
# Example SLURM header
#SBATCH --mem=64G
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=16
#SBATCH --time=04:00:00

Download scripts are provided at the start of each relevant tutorial.

title: Getting Started
text:
Recommended Project Structure
your-project/
├── data/
│   ├── raw/              # Cell Ranger output, fragment files
│   └── processed/        # .rds, .h5seurat, ArchR project
├── analysis/
│   ├── 01_qc.R
│   ├── 02_clustering.R
│   └── ...
├── figures/
├── renv.lock
└── README.md






TipSaving Seurat Objects Between Steps



Use saveRDS() and readRDS() to persist Seurat objects between chapters, avoiding re-running expensive computations:
# Save
saveRDS(seu, "data/processed/seu_02_normalized.rds")

# Load
seu <- readRDS("data/processed/seu_02_normalized.rds")

title: QC & Filtering
text:
Overview
scRNA-seq · Chapter 01
Raw single-cell RNA-seq data invariably contains low-quality cells — damaged cells with compromised membranes, empty droplets, or multiplets (two or more cells captured in one droplet). Retaining these artefacts distorts downstream clustering and differential expression results.
This chapter covers:

title: QC & Filtering
text:
Loading Cell Ranger output into a Seurat object
Computing per-cell QC metrics
Selecting filtering thresholds from QC distributions
Doublet detection with DoubletFinder
Saving the filtered object for normalization

title: QC & Filtering
text:
Setup

library(Seurat)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(dplyr)

title: QC & Filtering
text:
Load Data

# From Cell Ranger filtered output directory
counts <- Read10X(
  data.dir = "data/raw/pbmc3k/filtered_feature_bc_matrix/"
)

# --- OR from .h5 file ---
counts <- Read10X_h5("data/raw/pbmc3k/filtered_feature_bc_matrix.h5")

seu <- CreateSeuratObject(
  counts      = counts,
  project     = "PBMC3k",
  min.cells   = 3,    # retain genes detected in ≥ 3 cells
  min.features = 200  # retain cells with ≥ 200 genes detected
)

seu







Note



min.cells = 3 and min.features = 200 are deliberately loose. Stricter thresholds are applied after inspecting QC metric distributions.

title: QC & Filtering
text:
Compute QC Metrics

# Mitochondrial gene percentage — high % indicates damaged/dying cells
seu[["pct_mt"]] <- PercentageFeatureSet(seu, pattern = "^MT-")

# Ribosomal gene percentage — context-dependent, informative to track
seu[["pct_rb"]] <- PercentageFeatureSet(seu, pattern = "^RP[SL]")

# Preview metadata
head(seu@meta.data, 5)

title: QC & Filtering
text:
Visualise QC Distributions

VlnPlot(
  seu,
  features = c("nFeature_RNA", "nCount_RNA", "pct_mt"),
  ncol     = 3,
  pt.size  = 0.05,
  alpha    = 0.3
) & theme(axis.title.x = element_blank())


p1 <- FeatureScatter(seu, "nCount_RNA", "nFeature_RNA") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(200, 6000), linetype = "dashed", color = "#FF6B6B", linewidth = 0.6)

p2 <- FeatureScatter(seu, "nCount_RNA", "pct_mt") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 20, linetype = "dashed", color = "#FF6B6B", linewidth = 0.6)

p1 | p2

title: QC & Filtering
text:
Apply Filters

seu <- subset(
  seu,
  subset = nFeature_RNA > 200  &
           nFeature_RNA < 6000 &
           pct_mt < 20
)

cat("Cells retained:", ncol(seu), "\n")







WarningThreshold Selection is Dataset-Specific



The values above are appropriate for PBMC datasets. Adjust based on:

Cell type composition — neurons and hepatocytes naturally express more genes
Sequencing depth — deeper libraries shift the nCount_RNA distribution
Tissue of origin — immune cells have lower ambient RNA than solid tissues

title: QC & Filtering
text:
Doublet Detection
Doublets are droplets containing two cells that appear as one. Even after ambient RNA filtering, doublets can form distinct spurious clusters.

# DoubletFinder requires a preliminary normalization + PCA
# (these will be done properly in Chapter 02; here they are run solely for doublet detection)
seu <- NormalizeData(seu)
seu <- FindVariableFeatures(seu)
seu <- ScaleData(seu)
seu <- RunPCA(seu, npcs = 20)


library(DoubletFinder)

# Expected doublet rate: ~0.8% per 1,000 cells recovered (10x estimate)
nExp <- round(0.075 * ncol(seu))

seu <- doubletFinder(
  seu,
  PCs     = 1:20,
  pN      = 0.25,
  pK      = 0.09,   # pK should be optimised per sample — see ?paramSweep
  nExp    = nExp,
  reuse.pANN = FALSE
)

# Rename the classification column for clarity
seu$doublet_class <- seu[[grep("DF.classifications", colnames(seu@meta.data), value = TRUE)]]

# Remove doublets
seu <- subset(seu, subset = doublet_class == "Singlet")
cat("Singlets retained:", ncol(seu), "\n")







TipOptimising pK



Run paramSweep() followed by summarizeSweep() and find.pK() to determine the optimal pK value for your dataset. This adds ~5–10 minutes but meaningfully improves doublet detection accuracy.

title: QC & Filtering
text:
Summary Statistics

data.frame(
  Step       = c("After CreateSeuratObject", "After QC filter", "After doublet removal"),
  Cells      = c(ncol_raw, ncol_filtered, ncol(seu)),
  Median_nGene = c(
    median(raw_meta$nFeature_RNA),
    median(filt_meta$nFeature_RNA),
    median(seu$nFeature_RNA)
  )
)

title: QC & Filtering
text:
Save Checkpoint

saveRDS(seu, "data/processed/seu_01_filtered.rds")

title: Normalization & Feature Selection
text:
Overview
scRNA-seq · Chapter 02
Raw UMI counts are confounded by differences in sequencing depth across cells. Normalization removes this technical variation, allowing meaningful comparison of gene expression levels. Feature selection then focuses downstream analysis on genes that carry the most biological signal.
This chapter covers:

title: Normalization & Feature Selection
text:
Log-normalization (classical approach)
SCTransform v2 (variance-stabilizing approach)
Highly variable gene (HVG) selection
Data scaling

title: Normalization & Feature Selection
text:
Setup

library(Seurat)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)


seu <- readRDS("data/processed/seu_01_filtered.rds")

title: Normalization & Feature Selection
text:
Approach 1: Log-Normalization
The standard approach: normalize each cell to 10,000 counts (CPM-like), then apply a log1p transformation.

seu <- NormalizeData(
  seu,
  normalization.method = "LogNormalize",
  scale.factor         = 10000
)

title: Normalization & Feature Selection
text:
Approach 2: SCTransform v2 (Recommended)
SCTransform uses regularized negative binomial regression to model the mean-variance relationship, producing Pearson residuals that are largely independent of sequencing depth.

seu <- SCTransform(
  seu,
  vst.flavor          = "v2",         # use SCTransform v2
  vars.to.regress     = "pct_mt",     # optionally regress out mt percentage
  variable.features.n = 3000,
  verbose             = FALSE
)







TipWhich normalization should I use?




LogNormalize → faster, well-understood, compatible with all downstream Seurat functions
SCTransform v2 → generally better for datasets with high sequencing depth variance; required when integrating with SCTransform-normalized reference datasets

For most published workflows, SCTransform v2 is now the default recommendation.

title: Normalization & Feature Selection
text:
Highly Variable Gene Selection
HVGs are genes that vary more across cells than expected by chance — they capture biologically meaningful variation.

# If using LogNormalize (skip if using SCTransform — it selects HVGs internally)
seu <- FindVariableFeatures(
  seu,
  selection.method = "vst",
  nfeatures        = 3000
)

# Visualise top HVGs
top20 <- head(VariableFeatures(seu), 20)

p <- VariableFeaturePlot(seu)
LabelPoints(plot = p, points = top20, repel = TRUE, xnudge = 0, ynudge = 0)

title: Normalization & Feature Selection
text:
Scaling
Scaling centers each gene to mean 0 and variance 1 across cells, preventing highly expressed genes from dominating PCA.

# Scale only HVGs (faster; sufficient for PCA/UMAP)
seu <- ScaleData(seu, features = VariableFeatures(seu))

# --- OR scale all genes (required for heatmaps showing non-HVG genes) ---
seu <- ScaleData(seu, features = rownames(seu))







Note



When using SCTransform, scaling is performed internally. Calling ScaleData() again is not necessary for standard workflows.

title: Normalization & Feature Selection
text:
Save Checkpoint

saveRDS(seu, "data/processed/seu_02_normalized.rds")

title: Trajectory Analysis
text:
Overview
scRNA-seq · Chapter 07
Trajectory analysis orders cells along a continuum of developmental states, assigning each cell a pseudotime value that reflects its progress along a biological process (differentiation, activation, cell cycle, etc.).
This chapter covers:

title: Trajectory Analysis
text:
Converting a Seurat object to a Monocle3 cell_data_set
Preprocessing and dimensionality reduction within Monocle3
Learning the principal graph
Setting the root node and computing pseudotime
Identifying genes that change along pseudotime

title: Trajectory Analysis
text:
Setup

library(Seurat)
library(monocle3)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(dplyr)


seu <- readRDS("data/processed/seu_05_annotated.rds")

title: Trajectory Analysis
text:
Convert Seurat → Monocle3 cell_data_set

# Extract components from Seurat
gene_metadata <- data.frame(
  gene_short_name = rownames(seu),
  row.names       = rownames(seu)
)

cds <- new_cell_data_set(
  expression_data   = GetAssayData(seu, assay = "RNA", layer = "counts"),
  cell_metadata     = seu@meta.data,
  gene_metadata     = gene_metadata
)

title: Trajectory Analysis
text:
Preprocess and Reduce Dimensions

cds <- preprocess_cds(cds, num_dim = 30)
cds <- reduce_dimension(cds, reduction_method = "UMAP")







TipTransfer UMAP coordinates from Seurat



To keep your Seurat UMAP layout consistent with the trajectory plot, embed the Seurat UMAP coordinates directly into the Monocle3 object:
reducedDim(cds, type = "UMAP") <- Embeddings(seu, reduction = "umap")

title: Trajectory Analysis
text:
Cluster and Learn Graph

cds <- cluster_cells(cds, resolution = 1e-3)

cds <- learn_graph(
  cds,
  use_partition     = TRUE,
  close_loop        = FALSE,
  learn_graph_control = list(minimal_branch_len = 5)
)

plot_cells(
  cds,
  color_cells_by = "cell_type",
  label_groups_by_cluster = FALSE,
  label_leaves   = TRUE,
  label_branch_points = TRUE
)

title: Trajectory Analysis
text:
Order Cells in Pseudotime

# Interactive root selection (run in RStudio, not on HPC)
cds <- order_cells(cds)

# --- OR set root programmatically ---
# Identify the root node nearest to a known progenitor cluster
root_cells <- rownames(subset(colData(cds), cell_type == "HSC"))
cds <- order_cells(cds, root_cells = root_cells)

plot_cells(
  cds,
  color_cells_by = "pseudotime",
  label_cell_groups = FALSE,
  label_leaves   = FALSE,
  label_branch_points = FALSE,
  graph_label_size = 3
)

title: Trajectory Analysis
text:
Identify Pseudotime-Dependent Genes

# Test all genes (computationally intensive — subset to HVGs if needed)
pr_test <- graph_test(cds, neighbor_graph = "principal_graph", cores = 8)

pr_deg <- pr_test %>%
  filter(q_value < 0.05) %>%
  arrange(desc(morans_I))

head(pr_deg, 20)


# Group co-expressed genes into modules
gene_module_df <- find_gene_modules(
  cds[rownames(pr_deg)[1:500], ],
  resolution = c(10^seq(-6, -1))
)

plot_cells(
  cds,
  genes        = gene_module_df,
  label_cell_groups = FALSE,
  show_trajectory_graph = FALSE
)

title: Trajectory Analysis
text:
Save

saveRDS(cds, "data/processed/cds_07_trajectory.rds")

# Add pseudotime back to Seurat object
seu$pseudotime <- pseudotime(cds)[colnames(seu)]
saveRDS(seu, "data/processed/seu_07_pseudotime.rds")

title: Test
text:
Setup
Code
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)

title: Test
text:
Scatter Plot


Code
set.seed(42)
df <- data.frame(
  UMAP1  = c(rnorm(200, -2), rnorm(200, 2), rnorm(200, 0)),
  UMAP2  = c(rnorm(200, -2), rnorm(200, 2), rnorm(200, 3)),
  cluster = rep(c("T cell", "B cell", "Monocyte"), each = 200)
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = UMAP1, y = UMAP2, color = cluster)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.8, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "Simulated UMAP", color = "Cell Type")





Simulated UMAP-like scatter plot

title: Test
text:
Violin Plot


Code
df$expression <- c(
  abs(rnorm(200, 2, 1)),
  abs(rnorm(200, 4, 1)),
  abs(rnorm(200, 1, 0.5))
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = cluster, y = expression, fill = cluster)) +
  geom_violin(trim = FALSE, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.1, fill = "white", outlier.size = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "Expression by Cell Type", 
       x = NULL, y = "Normalized Expression") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")





Gene expression by cluster

title: Test
text:
Patchwork — combined plots


Code
p1 <- ggplot(df, aes(x = UMAP1, y = UMAP2, color = cluster)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.8, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

p2 <- ggplot(df, aes(x = cluster, y = expression, fill = cluster)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

p1 | p2





Combined layout with patchwork

title: Untitled
text:
Setup
library(Seurat)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(dplyr)
options(future.globals.maxSize = 100 * 1000 * 1024^2)
Extend limit to access to certain global variables
1GB = 1000 * 1024^2
Parallelization in Seurat with future
Code

Change the current plan to access parallelization
Code
plan("multicore", workers = 20)
plan()
Code
sample_name = "B9rxn1"

scRNA.cellranger_path = paste0("~/All_projects/cellranger_outputs/GRCh38_2024A/v901/", "CVc",
                               sample_name,
                               "_GEX_v901_ref2024A_SC5P-R2_R126-R290_cell23000/outs/filtered_feature_bc_matrix") 


scRNA.cellbender_path = paste0("~/All_projects/analysis_outputs/cellbender/GRCh38_ref2024A/learningrate1e-5/", "CVc",
                               sample_name, "/",
                               "CVc", sample_name, "_cb_v032_FPR_0.05_seurat.h5") 

scRNA.demux_path = paste0("~/All_projects/demuxlet_outputs/GRCh38_ref2024A/demuxlet_", "CVc",
                          sample_name,
                          "_GEX.best")

scRNA.input_path = paste0("~/sc_covidvac/inputs/Meta/subsetted/", 
                          "CVc_scRNA_metadata_", 
                          sample_name, ".csv")

scRNA.output_path = paste0("/mnt/vclab_storage_2025/scOmics/analysis_outputs/RNA/Preprocessing/")

############ Read table ############ 
scRNA.demux_meta <- read.table(scRNA.demux_path, header = T)
scRNA.demux_meta <- select(scRNA.demux_meta, c(BARCODE, DROPLET.TYPE, SNG.BEST.GUESS))

scRNA_meta <- read.csv(scRNA.input_path, header = T, row.names = 1)

#scRNA_meta$Sample_name[scRNA_meta$Sample_name == "Ref_Lonza"] 
scRNA.all_metadata <- merge(scRNA.demux_meta, scRNA_meta, by.x = "SNG.BEST.GUESS", by.y = "Sample_name")
rownames(scRNA.all_metadata) <-  scRNA.all_metadata$BARCODE
scRNA.all_metadata$BARCODE <- NULL

sample.batch_color <- scRNA.all_metadata$Batch_color[1]

title: Untitled
text:
Bypass argument when you running R script via bash script.
args <- commandArgs(trailingOnly = TRUE)
sample_name = args[1]

title: Untitled
text:
Load Data


Code
print("Load input data")
scRNA.data <- Read10X(scRNA.cellranger_path)

print("Load cellbender data")
scRNA.data_cb <- Read10X_h5(scRNA.cellbender_path, use.names = TRUE)

length(colnames(scRNA.data))
length(colnames(scRNA.data_cb))

scRNA.data_cb <- scRNA.data_cb[ ,colnames(scRNA.data)]
length(colnames(scRNA.data_cb))

print("Create Seurat object")
scRNA.obj <- CreateSeuratObject(scRNA.data_cb,
                                project = paste0(sample_name, "_RNA"),
                                min.cells = 0, min.features = 300,
                                meta.data = scRNA.all_metadata)


scRNA.data <- scRNA.data[ ,colnames(scRNA.obj)]
length(colnames(scRNA.data))
# create a new assay to store ADT information
ori_assay <- CreateAssay5Object(counts = scRNA.data)

# add this assay to the previously created Seurat object
scRNA.obj@assays[["ORI"]] <- ori_assay

# Validate that the object now contains multiple assays
Assays(scRNA.obj)
scRNA.obj




Code
DefaultAssay(scRNA.obj) <- "RNA"

#Idents(scRNA.obj) <- scRNA.obj$DROPLET.TYPE

#Idents(scRNA.obj) <- scRNA.obj$orig.ident

scRNA.obj[["percent.mt"]] <- PercentageFeatureSet(scRNA.obj, pattern = "^MT-")
scRNA.obj[["percent.rp"]] <- PercentageFeatureSet(scRNA.obj, pattern = "^RPS|^RPL")
scRNA.obj[["percent.hsp"]]  <- PercentageFeatureSet(scRNA.obj, pattern = "^HSP")

title: Signac: scRNA + scATAC Integration
text:
Overview
Multi-omics · Chapter 02
Signac extends Seurat to handle chromatin accessibility data. When paired with matched scRNA-seq (e.g., 10x Multiome), it enables joint analysis — linking open chromatin regions to gene expression at single-cell resolution.
This chapter covers:

title: Signac: scRNA + scATAC Integration
text:
Creating a Signac object from a 10x Multiome dataset
ATAC-seq QC within Signac
LSI dimensionality reduction
Joint WNN embedding with matched RNA
Linking peaks to genes

title: Signac: scRNA + scATAC Integration
text:
Setup

library(Seurat)
library(Signac)
library(EnsDb.Hsapiens.v86)
library(BSgenome.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg38)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)

title: Signac: scRNA + scATAC Integration
text:
Load 10x Multiome Data

# Read Cell Ranger ARC output
counts <- Read10X_h5("data/raw/pbmc_multiome/filtered_feature_bc_matrix.h5")

# Separate RNA and ATAC matrices
rna_counts  <- counts$`Gene Expression`
atac_counts <- counts$Peaks

# Load fragment file metadata
fragpath <- "data/raw/pbmc_multiome/atac_fragments.tsv.gz"

title: Signac: scRNA + scATAC Integration
text:
Create Seurat + Signac Object

# Genome annotation
annotation <- GetGRangesFromEnsDb(ensdb = EnsDb.Hsapiens.v86)
seqlevels(annotation) <- paste0("chr", seqlevels(annotation))

# Create chromatin assay
atac_assay <- CreateChromatinAssay(
  counts     = atac_counts,
  sep        = c(":", "-"),
  fragments  = fragpath,
  annotation = annotation
)

# Build Seurat object with both modalities
seu <- CreateSeuratObject(counts = rna_counts, assay = "RNA")
seu[["ATAC"]] <- atac_assay

seu

title: Signac: scRNA + scATAC Integration
text:
ATAC QC

DefaultAssay(seu) <- "ATAC"

seu <- NucleosomeSignal(seu)
seu <- TSSEnrichment(seu, fast = FALSE)

# Filter
seu <- subset(
  seu,
  subset = nCount_ATAC > 1000   &
           nCount_ATAC < 100000 &
           nCount_RNA  > 200    &
           nCount_RNA  < 25000  &
           nucleosome_signal < 2 &
           TSS.enrichment > 1
)


VlnPlot(
  seu,
  features = c("nCount_ATAC", "TSS.enrichment", "nucleosome_signal"),
  ncol = 3, pt.size = 0
)

title: Signac: scRNA + scATAC Integration
text:
Process Each Modality

DefaultAssay(seu) <- "RNA"
seu <- SCTransform(seu, verbose = FALSE)
seu <- RunPCA(seu, npcs = 30)


DefaultAssay(seu) <- "ATAC"
seu <- RunTFIDF(seu)
seu <- FindTopFeatures(seu, min.cutoff = "q0")
seu <- RunSVD(seu)   # LSI

title: Signac: scRNA + scATAC Integration
text:
Joint WNN Embedding

seu <- FindMultiModalNeighbors(
  seu,
  reduction.list = list("pca", "lsi"),
  dims.list      = list(1:30, 2:50),  # exclude LSI component 1 (correlated with depth)
  modality.weight.name = "RNA.weight"
)

seu <- RunUMAP(
  seu,
  nn.name    = "weighted.nn",
  reduction.name = "wnn.umap",
  reduction.key  = "wnnUMAP_"
)

seu <- FindClusters(seu, graph.name = "wsnn", resolution = 0.8)


p1 <- DimPlot(seu, reduction = "wnn.umap", label = TRUE) + NoLegend()
p2 <- FeaturePlot(seu, features = "RNA.weight", reduction = "wnn.umap")
p1 | p2

title: Signac: scRNA + scATAC Integration
text:
Link Peaks to Genes

DefaultAssay(seu) <- "ATAC"

seu <- LinkPeaks(
  seu,
  peak.assay = "ATAC",
  expression.assay = "SCT",
  genes.use  = VariableFeatures(seu, assay = "SCT")
)


CoveragePlot(
  seu,
  region     = "MS4A1",
  features   = "MS4A1",
  expression.assay = "SCT",
  extend.upstream   = 500,
  extend.downstream = 10000
)

title: Signac: scRNA + scATAC Integration
text:
Save

saveRDS(seu, "data/processed/seu_multiome_signac.rds")
